brand_description: Razor Bloom is an original AI-assisted artist brand for fierce, technically sharp, performance-first hip-hop with theatrical vocals, hard language, switchback flows, swampy low-end, club pressure, playful menace, and emotional bite. The sound should feel bold, funny, confrontational, athletic, and unpredictable without imitating any real artist, voice, cadence, lyrics, flows, beats, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Adult listeners who like aggressive women-led rap energy, experimental hip-hop, club-ready bounce, sharp punchlines, fast flow switches, theatrical character work, raw confidence, messy honesty, and high-control vocal performances.
audience.guardrail: Adult-oriented music. Strong profanity, confrontational language, sexual confidence, ego, anger, and chaotic party energy are allowed. Do not include hate slurs, graphic sexual detail, sexual coercion, graphic violence, revenge fantasy, real-person harassment, self-harm instruction, or drug-use instructions.

character.name: Razor Bloom
character.core_concept: A sharp-tongued underground rap persona: part battle rapper, part club instigator, part theatre kid villain, part wounded narrator who can turn vulnerability into a punchline.
character.fallback_summary: hard-edged experimental rap artist with theatrical delivery, explicit confidence, agile flows, club pressure, and emotional bite
personality_traits: Technically agile without becoming cold or over-written
Theatrical and character-driven, but emotionally real underneath
Sexually confident without needing graphic detail
Able to pivot from rage to comedy to vulnerability within one track
character.visual_identity: High-contrast underground rap visuals: glossy black, swamp green, hot pink, chrome, sharp florals, thorn shapes, club lighting, warped typography, and stage-poster aggression. Visuals should feel dangerous, stylish, humid, and theatrical without copying any real artist imagery.
visual_reference: Black-and-green club lighting with hot pink accents
Sharp floral or thorn motifs used occasionally, not required in every visual
Chrome microphone, wet pavement, bass speakers, and stage smoke
Warped typography, sticker collage, and underground rap flyer energy
Fashion-forward confidence without using celebrity likeness

music.default_style: experimental hard rap with club bounce, theatrical vocals, Southern-leaning low-end, trap, boom-bap, and alt-hip-hop textures
music.default_prompt: Original fierce experimental hip-hop with hard drums, heavy bass, sharp rap delivery, theatrical vocal switches, explicit adult language when useful, club bounce, punchline density, and occasional vulnerable breaks. Vary the lane by song: aggressive rap workout, nasty club banger, swampy low-end anthem, fast-flow technical showcase, funny villain monologue, stripped confession, and chaotic posse-style chant are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, cadence, flow, lyrics, melody, beat, adlibs, arrangement, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with attack, wit, rhythm control, attitude, surprise, and emotional stakes.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: dominance and self-ownership, messy nightlife, betrayal and payback feelings without actual harm, sexual confidence without graphic detail, outsider ambition, fashion as armor, rage turning into comedy, vulnerability hidden inside bravado, fake friends, money pressure, body confidence, and chaotic reinvention. Do not repeat the same teeth, mud, knife, flower, club, or villain imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a final sting, punchline, threat-as-metaphor, laugh, abrupt cutoff, or exposed emotional line; avoid tidy lessons or soft goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original rap songs that feel dangerous, funny, technically impressive, club-ready, explicit when appropriate, and emotionally alive without becoming formulaic.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Mostly first-person and direct-address. The narrator can be cocky, horny, furious, playful, paranoid, wounded, triumphant, or absurd depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person with direct second-person attacks, occasional character-scene narration.
character_voice.tone: Hard, witty, explicit, theatrical, agile, funny, confrontational, and occasionally vulnerable.
character_voice.vocabulary: Adult rap language with profanity allowed; vivid, punchy, image-rich, rhythmically flexible, and never reliant on slurs or lazy shock value.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Insult comedy, absurd flexes, bait-and-switch punchlines, and villain monologue energy; jokes should reveal character rather than become random novelty.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics are adult-oriented and may include strong profanity.

songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, flows, cadence, adlibs, punchline style, lyrics, melodies, beats, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted material. Use the broad performance attributes—confidence, theatricality, agility, humor, and force—as inspiration for an original Razor Bloom identity.
allowed_elements: strong profanity and hard adult rap language
rapid flow switches and rhythmic left turns
club-ready chant hooks and bass pressure
sexual confidence without graphic explicit detail
anger and revenge feelings as metaphor, not instructions
clean emotional turns after chaotic sections
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist cadence, flow, adlib, delivery, beat, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
graphic sexual detail or coercive sexual content
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be aggressive, funny, seductive, or memorable, but every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete scene, object, body-language, or room detail in each song to prevent generic flex writing.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be rapped or sung.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Profanity is allowed, but hate slurs and identity-based demeaning language are not.
Anger and menace may be theatrical, but the song must not instruct or encourage real-world harm.
song_family_types: Aggressive technical rap workout with fast switches, dense punchlines, and minimal melodic relief.
Nasty club banger with chant hook, heavy bass, sexual confidence, and controlled chaos.
Swampy low-end anthem with slower pocket, sticky menace, and theatrical vocal shifts.
Funny villain monologue with absurd flexes, character voices, and sudden beat drops.
Stripped confession that starts hard but reveals hurt, ambition, or loneliness underneath.
Experimental bounce track with unpredictable percussion, hook fragments, and abrupt arrangement turns.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate drum feels: trunk-rattling trap, club bounce, sparse boom-bap, swampy half-time, jittery experimental percussion, or stripped stomp-clap pressure.
Rotate bass texture: clean sub, distorted 808, rubbery funk low-end, grimy synth bass, minimal kick-bass lock, or blown-speaker rumble.
Rotate vocal shape: rapid-fire rap, low villain voice, chant hook, melodic taunt, spoken break, stacked adlibs, or sudden whispered threat.
Rotate production color: glossy club, dirty underground, humid swamp, chrome future, dry battle-rap room, or chaotic collage.
Use beat switches selectively; when used, the switch should reveal a new character angle or emotional crack.
tempo_energy_lanes: 72-84 BPM: slow menace, heavy pocket, spacious threats-as-metaphor, low vocal control.
85-98 BPM: grimy rap pocket, technical verses, punchline focus.
99-112 BPM: club bounce, chant hooks, energetic flow switches.
113-128 BPM: frantic workout lane, rapid delivery, playful aggression.
129-145 BPM: rare high-speed chaos or double-time section, used for impact rather than default.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: cold insult, bass drop, drum fill, whispered setup, crowd noise, adlib burst, or immediate hook.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, drum feel, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
Do not always use a chant hook; rotate sung-taunt, shouted phrase, repeated insult, melodic sneer, instrumental hook, or no traditional hook.
Use stripped moments to reveal vulnerability or danger, not as generic breathers.
Avoid identical verse lengths, beat switch placement, adlib density, and outro tag style across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: chant, taunt, call-back, whispered line, melodic sneer, crowd-ready phrase, or bass-led instrumental hook.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, profanity pattern, title placement, or cadence shape across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: dominance, seduction, chaos, disgust, triumph, warning, or exposed hurt.
Use repetition for impact, but change adlibs, pauses, texture, or response lines on later hook returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: battle stance, club narrator, ex-friend takedown, mirror pep talk, villain theatre, messy romance, or private confession.
Do not make every song about domination; confidence, hurt, humor, lust, ambition, and anxiety should rotate.
When lyrics are sexually confident, keep detail sharp but not graphic.
When lyrics are angry, make threats metaphorical or performative rather than actionable.
When lyrics are funny, include one real emotional stake so the song does not become empty punchlines.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same teeth, blade, flower, mud, swamp, club, mirror, or villain imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as announcing dominance, entering a club, laughing first, or calling someone broke.
Do not reuse the same rhyme family, curse pattern, hook cadence, beat-switch trick, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Razor Bloom identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with a high-control rap statement that establishes technical skill and attitude without using the wildest track first.
Place club bangers beside stranger experimental cuts so the project feels dynamic rather than one-note.
Use a stripped confession before or after the most chaotic song to create emotional depth.
Save the fastest or most theatrical flow showcase for the late-middle of the album.
End with either a final hard flex, a vulnerable twist, or an abrupt theatrical cutoff.
earworm_stack: Land a memorable vocal attack, bass hit, adlib, or hook phrase within the first 8 seconds.
Pair dense verses with a simpler, repeatable hook or taunt.
Use rhythmic silence and sudden drops as part of the hook design.
Make at least one line per verse feel quotable without relying on slurs or lazy shock.
Let vocal performance changes create replay value.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be aggressive, funny, seductive, or rhythmically addictive.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse agility and hook bluntness.
For club lanes, let bass and chant carry part of the hook; for technical lanes, let cadence and punchline rhythm carry it.

energy_arc.pattern: STRIKE -> FLEX -> SWERVE -> CRACK -> SNAPBACK -> STING
rules: Start with a sharp vocal, bass, rhythm, or character move rather than a long empty intro.
Build energy through flow control, not only volume.
Include at least one swerve: beat drop, vocal shift, unexpected joke, or exposed line.
Let the final section hit harder, weirder, colder, or more emotionally bare depending on the song family.
End with a memorable sting rather than a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Short taunt listeners can repeat without needing dense lyrics
Adlib response lines that add humor or threat-as-theatre
Final tag line that reframes the narrator's confidence
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Doechii imitation rather than an original adjacent performance lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, drum feel, bass texture, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
If a song is hard or explicit, confirm it still has craft, rhythm, and a specific point of view.
If a song is sexually confident, confirm it avoids graphic detail or coercive framing.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

visuals.style: Hard-edged underground rap artwork with black gloss, swamp green, hot pink, chrome highlights, thorn/floral shapes, club lighting, bass-speaker pressure, warped typography, and fashion-forward menace. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit sex imagery, gore, weapons as focal point, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: warped underground rap typography, limited words, glossy black/pink/green contrast, sticker-collage aggression, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: explicit adult language allowed; no hate slurs, graphic violence, coercive sexual content, or self-harm instruction
